--- SOURCE 1: Advancements in Surveying and GIS for Disaster Resilience ---

SUMMARY:

AI enhances GIS predictive modeling (Anwar, et al. disaster resilience by providing accurate, real-time data for advancing GIS applications in disaster resilience.

FULL CONTENT:
Impact Factor : 0.548



Case StudyOpen Access

Advancements in Surveying and GIS for Disaster Resilience: Protecting Public Health in Vulnerable
Regions
 Volume 60- Issue 5

Mostafa Abdel Bary Ebrahim*

Received: March 03, 2025;  Published: March 13, 2025

*Corresponding author: Mostafa Abdel Bary Ebrahim, Civil and Environmental Department, Faculty of Engineering King AbdulAziz
University, Saudi Arabia

DOI:  10.26717/BJSTR.2025.60.009530

Abstract



         PDF

ABSTRACT

Disasters pose significant threats to public health, particularly in vulnerable regions with geographic risks and
socio-economic challenges. This paper explores advancements in surveying and Geographic Information Systems
(GIS) that enhance disaster resilience. By leveraging high-resolution remote sensing, LiDAR, real-time GIS
platforms, and predictive modeling, these technologies support disaster preparedness, response, and recovery
efforts. Their applications in risk assessment, emergency healthcare access, disease prevention, and resilient
infrastructure underscore their role in protecting public health. However, challenges such as data accuracy, technological
barriers, and privacy concerns must be addressed. This paper highlights case studies demonstrating
the transformative impact of surveying and GIS on disaster resilience and public health protection. Future directions
include AI integration, enhanced data sharing mechanisms, and cross-disciplinary research to optimize GIS
applications in disaster resilience.

Abbreviations: MCDA: Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis; GIS: Geographic Information Systems; RS: Remote Sensing;
IWR: Integrated Water Resources; LiDAR: Light Detection and Ranging

Introduction

Disasters, both natural and human-induced, significantly disrupt
public health, especially in regions with pre-existing vulnerabilities.
The increasing frequency and severity of disasters, driven by climate
change and urban expansion, disproportionately impact vulnerable
regions, disrupting public health through disease, injury, and infrastructure
failure (IPCC [1]). Coastal zones, seismic areas, and underserved
communities face elevated risks due to limited resources
and outdated tools (Cutter, et al. [2]). Surveying and GIS technologies
have emerged as essential tools for enhancing disaster resilience by
providing real-time data, predictive modeling, and spatial analysis to
bolster disaster resilience (Goodchild [3]). GIS also supports longterm
urban planning and climate adaptation, as governments rely
on these tools for policymaking (UN-Habitat [4]). This paper examines
how these advancements safeguard public health by enhancing
preparedness, response, and recovery, using verified global examples
and addressing implementation challenges through risk assessment,
emergency response, and disease control. Moreover, the role of GIS in
disaster management extends beyond immediate response to longterm
urban planning and policymaking. Governments and organizations
increasingly rely on these technologies for climate adaptation
strategies, making it crucial to examine their broader implications.

Technological Advancements in Surveying and GIS

Recent advancements in surveying and GIS have transformed disaster
management. These technologies provide critical data to improve
disaster preparedness, response, and recovery by delivering
accurate, actionable data for disaster management.

High-Resolution Remote Sensing

Satellites and drones provide high-resolution imagery for real-
time monitoring of disaster affected areas (Lillesand, et al. [5]).
Landslides are present on all continents and play an important role
in the evolution of landscapes. They also represent a serious hazard
in many areas of the world (Guzzetti, et al. [6]). This supports damage
assessments and tracks changes like flood extents or wildfire spread
(USGS [7]). Additionally, Hyperspectral imaging enhances detection
of hazards such as oil spills post-disaster (Jensen [8]).

LiDAR and Photogrammetry

LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) creates precise 3D terrain
models, which are vital for mapping flood risks and assessing seismic
structural integrity (Carter, et al. [9]). Photogrammetry offers cost-effective
alternatives, aiding disaster-prone areas (Westoby, et al. [10]).
These tools are instrumental in mapping flood risks, predicting landslides,
and evaluating earthquake-resistant structures. Recent studies
highlight how LiDAR data integration with AI models enhances real-
time landslide prediction accuracy. AI integration with LiDAR improves
landslide prediction (Stephen, et al. [11]).

Real-Time GIS Platforms

Real-time GIS, linked with Internet of Things (IoT) sensors and
mobile devices, produces dynamic disaster maps for coordination
(Esri, 2021). Emergency responders utilize these platforms to coordinate
efforts, track resources, and adapt to evolving disaster conditions.
The integration of GIS with blockchain technology ensures
secure and transparent data sharing across emergency response networks.
Blockchain ensures secure data sharing across response networks
(Reyna, et al. [12]).

Spatial Analysis and Predictive Modeling

Advanced GIS tools analyze spatial data to predict disaster impacts
and optimize planning (Longley, et al. [13]). Predictive models
identify vulnerable populations, assess potential disaster impacts,
and determine strategic locations for emergency shelters and medical
facilities. The increasing adoption of AI-powered GIS has led to
significant improvements in hazard forecasting. AI enhances hazard
forecasting accuracy, identifying vulnerable populations and shelter
locations (Chen, et al. [12]).

Integration with Early Warning Systems

GIS enhances early warning systems by integrating hazard data
with spatial analysis, improving evacuation planning and timely
alerts, minimizing casualties and reducing health risks (UNDRR [14]).
Advances in crowd-sourced GIS data collection have improved the accuracy
and timeliness of disaster alerts, particularly in low-resource
settings (Meier [15]). This enhances timely interventions, reducing
disaster-related health risks.

Protecting Public Health Vulnerable Regions

Surveying and GIS address disaster-related public health risks.
The ultimate goal of utilizing surveying and GIS technologies in disaster
resilience is to protect public health. These tools enhance risk
assessment, healthcare accessibility, disease control, and infrastructure
resilience.

Risk Assessment and Vulnerability Mapping

GIS maps demographic data including age, income, and disability
status with hazard zones to identify at-risk groups populations (Tate,
et al. [16]). Authorities use this information to allocate resources effectively,
reducing disaster-related morbidity and mortality rates.
Multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) techniques enhance vulnerability
mapping by integrating diverse datasets for resource allocation
(Malczewski [17]).

Emergency Response and Healthcare Access

Real-time GIS optimizes ambulance routes, locates temporary
medical sites, and coordinates relief efforts. (Russ [18]). These applications
ensure timely medical assistance and continuity of healthcare
services during disasters. Advanced Spatial analytics ensure timely
supply distribution by facilitating the real-time distribution of medical
supplies to critical zones (WHO [19]).

Disease Prevention and Control

Surveying tracks environmental factors, while GIS maps disease
vectors such as water contamination and mosquito breeding sites,
which contribute to disease outbreaks post-disaster (WHO [20]).
GIS-driven epidemiological models are increasingly used to predict
disease spread such as cholera mapping in flood zones (Hay, et al.
[21]).

Resilient Infrastructure

LiDAR and GIS assess infrastructure vulnerabilities, ensuring
health service continuity (Linda [22]). This aids long-term recovery
planning.

Case Studies

1. Bangladesh (Cyclone Preparedness): LiDAR and satellite imagery
improved floodplain mapping, while GIS optimized shelter
placement, reducing cyclone mortality. Health facilities were prioritized
using vulnerability data (Mallick, et al. [23]).

2. California (Wildfires): Drone surveys and GIS tracked the 2018
Camp Fire, enabling evacuations and air quality alerts (CAL FIRE
[24]).

3. Sub-Saharan Africa (Drought): GIS integrated satellite data
with health records to target food aid, combating malnutrition
(FAO [25]).

4. Saudi Arabia (Water Demand): The use of new techniques
such as Remote Sensing (RS) and Geographic Information Systems
(GIS) are currently considered as powerful tools within Integrated
Water Resources (IWR) planning and management. The
main objective of the project was to provide a methodology for
using such techniques for counting, representing, and classifying
crops for estimation of the agriculture demand for water in KSA.
A case study is provided to demonstrate this methodology based
on technical presentation of a project in KSA (Ebrahim [26]).
This methodology supports drought resilience, mitigating water
scarcity’s impact on public health. These examples collectively
demonstrate GIS’s versatility across diverse hazard types and socioeconomic
contexts and health challenges.

Challenges and Limitations

Despite their advantages, surveying and GIS technologies face
challenges in disaster resilience efforts. For instance, integrating engineers’
structural data with epidemiologists’ disease models remains
challenging due to differing data standards.

• Data Accuracy and Accessibility: Inaccurate or outdated data
can undermine GIS reliability, particularly in remote or developing
regions (UNDRR [27]).

• Technological Barriers: Advanced GIS tools require infrastructure
and training, which may be limited in vulnerable areas (Tomaszewski
[28]).

• Interdisciplinary Collaboration: Effective disaster management
requires coordination across engineering, health, and
policy sectors, which is often hindered by siloed expertise (NRC
[29]).

• Privacy Concerns: The collection of geospatial data raises ethical
issues related to surveillance and data consent (Elwood, et
al. [30]).

Future Directions

AI enhances GIS predictive modeling (Anwar, et al. [31]), blockchain
improves data sharing (Reyna, et al. [12]), and crowd-sourcing
boosts real-time alerts (Meier [15]). Expanding training and platforms
like OpenStreetMap could democratize GIS access will improve
accessibility (UNDRR [14]). Interdisciplinary research will optimize
resilience strategies.

Conclusion

Advancements in surveying and GIS technologies are revolutionizing
disaster resilience by providing accurate, real-time data
that enhances emergency preparedness and public health protection.
These tools are instrumental in risk assessment, emergency
response optimization, disease prevention, and the development of
resilient infrastructure. As disasters become more frequent and severe
due to climate change and urbanization, the integration of GIS
with AI, blockchain, and real-time data analytics will further enhance
its impact. Despite the promising potential of these technologies,
challenges such as data accessibility, technological limitations, and
ethical considerations must be addressed to maximize their effectiveness.
Strengthening interdisciplinary collaboration among engineers,
healthcare professionals, policymakers, and data scientists is crucial
for advancing GIS applications in disaster resilience. Future research
should focus on improving the accuracy and interoperability of geospatial
data, expanding training initiatives for emergency responders,
and developing frameworks to balance data privacy with the need for
effective disaster management. By leveraging these advancements,
societies can build more resilient communities capable of mitigating
disaster risks and ensuring public health protection in the face of
evolving global challenges. health protection. Future research should
focus on AI integration with GIS for improved predictive capabilities.

Additionally, strengthening interdisciplinary collaboration, enhancing
data accessibility, and addressing ethical considerations will
maximize the potential of these technologies in disaster resilience. By
bridging civil engineering, geospatial science, and public health, these
technologies pave the way for safer and more resilient communities.
Ultimately, these advancements position GIS as a cornerstone of global
disaster resilience in an increasingly volatile world.

Mapping the Future of Disaster Management: The Role of GIS
==========================================================

*   ![Image 2: Writer: Rajimol TR](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/5d1736_f09760c245e041279021be91b1993496%7Emv2.jpeg/v1/fill/w_32,h_32,al_c,q_80,enc_avif,quality_auto/5d1736_f09760c245e041279021be91b1993496%7Emv2.jpeg) [Rajimol TR](https://www.agsrt.com/profile/46079fee-d6e3-4707-b059-56adc85ab738/profile)
*   Sep 19, 2024
*   4 min read

#### **Introduction**

Geographical Information Systems (GIS) have emerged as an important tool in handling the disaster management, offering a major approach to understanding, mitigating, and responding to natural hazards.

GIS has become a significant tool in hazard mapping, vulnerability assessment, and emergency response preparedness. GIS facilitates hazard mapping by integrating diverse spatial datasets, including topography, land use, and historical hazard occurrences. It also facilitates a platform to find high-risk areas prone to floods, earthquakes, Tsunami, and other hazards, thus helps the policymakers to prioritize resources for mitigation efforts and land-use planning.

#### **Disaster Management**

Disaster is an event which causes high damage for the environment, properties and human life. Disaster Management is the science and technology for the proper planning and execution of tacking the disasters. The main aim of the disaster management is the prevention and loss deduction.

Disaster management activity is done through the phases of:

*   Prediction

*   Prevention

*   Mitigation

*   Emergency Management

*   Recovery

The applications of past data and geographical concepts and disaster management solutions can help the communities to manage risks effectively. It can be done through:

**Situational Awareness:** Remote sensing, or the use of satellite imagery and aerial photography provides up-to-date and high-resolution visual data of the affected areas. By integrating this data with GIS, emergency response teams can quickly assess the extent of the disaster, identify impacted areas, and monitor the progression of the event. This situational awareness helps in making informed decisions and allocating resources effectively.

**Damage Assessment:** Geoinformatics can aid in rapid damage assessment after a natural disaster. By comparing pre-disaster and post-disaster satellite images or aerial photographs, emergency response teams can identify areas of destruction, assess the severity of damage to infrastructure (buildings, roads, bridges), and prioritize rescue and recovery efforts accordingly. This information aids in resource allocation and planning for reconstruction.

**Hazard Mapping and Prediction:** GIS can do hazard mapping and prediction. By analyzing historical data, topography, and other relevant factors, It can identify areas at high risk of natural disasters, such as flood-prone zones or landslide-prone areas. Remote sensing data, including weather patterns and satellite imagery, is crucial for predicting and tracking the progression of natural events, such as hurricanes, storms, or floods. This information assists emergency response teams in issuing timely warnings and implementing evacuation plans.

**Evacuation Planning and Route Optimization:** GIS plays a main role in evacuation planning and route optimization. By considering factors like population density, road network, topography, and predicted disaster impact areas, GIS can help identify evacuation routes, determine capacity of evacuation centers, and estimate transportation requirements. It assists emergency response teams in efficiently evacuating people from high-risk areas, ensuring their safety.

**Resource Management:** GIS is very efficient in resource management during emergency response. By integrating data on available resources (e.g., medical facilities, emergency supplies, temporary shelters) and overlaying it with geospatial information, emergency response teams can quickly identify the nearest resources to affected areas and allocate them accordingly. GIS helps in coordinating logistics, optimizing resource distribution, and minimizing response time.

**Communication and Coordination:** GIS helps to do communication and coordination among emergency response teams. They enable real-time sharing of geospatial information, such as situation maps, evacuation plans, and resource locations. GIS allows different response teams and agencies to collaborate, exchange data, and make informed decisions based on a common operational picture.

GIS is a highly applied technology for capturing the disaster vulnerability zones, storing the data related to location, checking the data with other layers, and displaying disaster related data related to the particular area of the earth surface.

GIS can show many different kinds of data related with disasters on one map, such as streets, buildings, and vegetation, Land use / Land cover, Geology and so on. This enables people to more easily see, analyze, and understand patterns and relationships between these phenomena.

![Image 3: GIS](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/5d1736_8639f945d52c4db2a8ef6672f88c6203~mv2.jpg/v1/fill/w_350,h_296,al_c,q_80,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,enc_avif,quality_auto/5d1736_8639f945d52c4db2a8ef6672f88c6203~mv2.jpg)

##### **GIS in tackling Flood:**

GIS based Maps are created for mitigating the flood contain the information like Flood affected areas, Drainage networks, Population density distribution, villages or areas having Road connectivity, hospital and clinics situated near flood affected areas. Route map for the disaster-prone areas, Villages without proper road connectivity can be identified.

![Image 4: GIS in tackling Flood](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/5d1736_c0b068d21887470591e2b978f9803be2~mv2.jpg/v1/fill/w_740,h_452,al_c,q_80,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,enc_avif,quality_auto/5d1736_c0b068d21887470591e2b978f9803be2~mv2.jpg)

##### **Tsunami Risk Analysis**

A Geographic Information System can detect, locate, monitor the potentially tsunamic events occurring in the oceans. It can also provide storm surge advisories with storm surge heights and inundation extent along the coastal regions. Disaster management technique through GIS can make the coastal community to be prepared for Tsunami emergencies, minimize the loss of life and property, and can ensure structural and systematic approach for creating the community for disaster preparedness.

![Image 5: Tsunami Risk Analysis](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/5d1736_96bf3f00bc184291ab0f0d8ee26048c7~mv2.jpg/v1/fill/w_350,h_294,al_c,q_80,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,enc_avif,quality_auto/5d1736_96bf3f00bc184291ab0f0d8ee26048c7~mv2.jpg)

##### **GIS in the management of Earth quakes**

-GIS approach is applied for the identification of earthquake potential areas. Important factors related to earthquakes can be recognized and relative input data layers such as Digital Elevation Model, slope, earthquake magnitude, epicenter location, lineaments, faults, distance to active faults and epicenter) are developed to for the disaster mitigation.

-The satellite images such as Sentinel-2 and data from Shuttle Radar Topography Mission (SRTM) earthquake data, and geological data are used in GIS for disaster management.

-For data integration in GIS, a numerical ranking scheme can be adopted to establish rank values for each factor for the appraisal of earthquake potential index (EPI) map. The final earthquake potential index map can classify the study region into different corresponding potential classes such as high, moderate, low, and very low. The earthquake potential map produced for any region can be compared with the previous seismic hazard maps derived from traditional techniques. The use of various parameters and implementation of the suggested method in a particular region using GIS can produce Earth quake Disaster mitigation and management plans.

![Image 6: GIS in the management of Earth quakes](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/5d1736_2661b01bac4e4451ada6a173ee403061~mv2.jpg/v1/fill/w_740,h_523,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,enc_avif,quality_auto/5d1736_2661b01bac4e4451ada6a173ee403061~mv2.jpg)

#### **Conclusion**

GIS provides mechanism for integrating and analyzing data from various sources across time, for planners, decision makers, Government and so on. GIS is a crucial technology for emergency management, it has the ability to transform potential disasters in order to solve severe issues and reduce catastrophic repercussions.

--- SOURCE 3: GIS in Disaster Management | Emergency Management Operations ---
URL: https://www.esri.com/en-us/industries/emergency-management/overview

SUMMARY:
# Emergency and Disaster Management Emergency managers use geographic information system (GIS) technology with data-driven insights to better prepare and help their communities respond and recover from significant events. GIS builds shared situational awareness across response teams, stakeholders, and the public. Using GIS expedites response and recovery efforts, monitors the status of community lifelines, and helps you communicate recovery resources to the public. ## GIS for Emergency Management ebook ## Emergency and Disaster Management solutions Temporarily extend existing licenses or gain access to ArcGIS, the world's leading GIS technology, to support GIS requirements during a disaster response. Request immediate assistance for an active emergency response or find out how we help when disaster strikes.

FULL CONTENT:
![](/content/dam/esrisites/en-us/industries/2022/public-safety/emergency-management/assets/overview/industry-emergency-management-overview-banner-medium-background.jpg)

# Emergency and Disaster Management

Build resilience, respond, and recover with real-time GIS technology

![A map with highlighted hotspots, sandbags holding bag water, and a dispatcher holding a phone and a radio](/content/dam/esrisites/en-us/industries/2022/public-safety/emergency-management/assets/overview/emergency-management-overview-foreground.png)

Focusing efforts to mitigate the impacts and protect against the complex threats and hazards in today's modern world requires a deeper understanding of the social, economic, and environmental systems around us. Emergency managers use geographic information system (GIS) technology with data-driven insights to better prepare and help their communities respond and recover from significant events.

### Build resilience with data-driven insight

Understand, identify, and build strategies that mitigate natural hazards and human-caused threats using the science of geography. Using GIS, areas of risk and vulnerability are highlighted by combining critical infrastructure, hazard, and demographics data into a single map.

### Respond with real-time information

Anticipate needs during rapidly changing conditions with real-time dashboards that aid critical decision-making and maximize the effectiveness of emergency management operations. GIS builds shared situational awareness across response teams, stakeholders, and the public.

### Reestablish lifelines with mobile tools

Conduct damage assessments, build a reporting database, and monitor debris removal with field data collecting and reporting applications. Using GIS expedites response and recovery efforts, monitors the status of community lifelines, and helps you communicate recovery resources to the public.

## US Coast Guard drives critical bridge response with GIS

USCG's geospatial data-driven response reopened Baltimore's port in 76 days, showcasing interagency collaboration and technology use.

![Panorama view of a bustling modern riverside city beginning to light up for nighttime under a soft orange and blue sunset sky](/content/dam/esrisites/en-us/industries/2022/public-safety/emergency-management/assets/overview/uscg-key-bridge-response-50-50-split.jpg)

## GIS for Emergency Management ebook

This ebook describes how to leverage data and location intelligence for analysis, improve emergency response and recovery outcomes, and effectively utilize data to build long-term resilience.

![Heat map of a sprawling river system with many small tributaries snaking out from the main body of water](/content/dam/esrisites/en-us/industries/2022/public-safety/emergency-management/assets/overview/em-ebook-50-50-split.jpg)

## Emergency and Disaster Management solutions

### Disaster Resilience

### Situational Awareness

### Damage Assessment

### Debris Management

### Information Sharing

## Esri Disaster Response Program (DRP)

Esri was founded to help solve some of the world's most difficult problems. For over 25 years, the Esri DRP has provided GIS support to our users and the global community during disasters and crises of all types and sizes.

![A pink and purple sunset shining on the clouds and across a plain](/content/dam/esrisites/en-us/industries/2022/public-safety/emergency-management/assets/overview/industry-emergency-management-overview-two-thirds.jpg)

### ArcGIS software

Temporarily extend existing licenses or gain access to ArcGIS, the world's leading GIS technology, to support GIS requirements during a disaster response.

### Workflow implementation

Discover modern capabilities to support disaster response. We can implement or help you configure battle-tested solutions during your response.

### Geospatial data

Respond with incident-specific data—such as real-time weather, traffic, hazards, infrastructure information, and demographics—from the response community and private sector.

### Technical support

Gain access to our industry-leading premium support services. A dedicated team of professionals is ready to address any question or issue related to ArcGIS technology.

## Upcoming events

Stay up-to-date on trends, technology, and best practices related to GIS in public safety.

![Person wearing glasses, seated at a desk, smiling and looking at a laptop screen](/content/dam/esrisites/en-us/industries/2021/public-safety/wildland-fire/assets/overview/wildland-fire-mts-webinar.jpg)

# GIS in the Field - The Role of Geographic Information Science in Natural Disaster Response

![GisC in the field blog header image](https://static.everspring-kent.production.k2.m1.brightspot.cloud/dims4/default/99e4338/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1874x464+0+0/resize/1440x357!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fk2-prod-everspring-kent.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9f%2F5a%2F7de6fff64fb8b676f22bd3735b08%2Fscreenshot-2025-01-18-at-8-56-33-pm.png)

Natural disasters like hurricanes, wildfires, and earthquakes can devastate communities, but Geographic Information Science (GISc) plays a critical role in preparing for, responding to, and recovering from these events. By leveraging spatial data and advanced technology, GIS professionals provide actionable insights that save lives, protect property, and guide recovery efforts.

Through [Kent State University’s 100% online Master of Geographic Information Science program](https://onlinedegrees.kent.edu/geography/geographic-information-science/overview), students gain the skills to make a tangible impact in this crucial field. Let’s explore how GIS is transforming disaster response and how this program equips you to contribute to this vital work.

# **Preparing for Disasters with GIS**

Effective disaster response starts long before an event occurs. GIS technology helps agencies analyze historical data, identify at-risk areas, and develop strategies to minimize harm. For example:

With this predictive power, GIS professionals play an essential role in reducing disaster impacts and protecting vulnerable populations.

## **Real-Time Disaster Response**

During a disaster, time is of the essence. GIS professionals provide real-time data that emergency teams use to make informed decisions, such as:

This ability to provide actionable insights under pressure demonstrates the life-saving potential of GIS.

## **Supporting Recovery Efforts**

The role of GIS doesn’t end when a disaster subsides. In the recovery phase, GIS professionals help rebuild communities by:

## **How Kent State Prepares You for GIS in Disaster Response**

[Kent State University’s online Master of Geographic Information Science program](https://onlinedegrees.kent.edu/geography/geographic-information-science/overview) equips students with the technical and analytical skills needed to excel in roles that impact disaster management. Here’s how:

## **A Career That Makes a Difference**

With the increasing frequency of natural disasters, GIS professionals are in high demand. Careers in disaster response include roles in government agencies, nonprofit organizations, and private firms. By earning your [online Master of Geographic Information Science from Kent State](https://onlinedegrees.kent.edu/geography/geographic-information-science/overview), you’ll be prepared to step into a rewarding career that combines cutting-edge technology with meaningful impact.

## **Start Making an Impact Today**

Are you ready to use GIS to make a difference in disaster response and recovery? Learn more about Kent State’s online Master of Geographic Information Science program at [Kent State Online](https://onlinedegrees.kent.edu/geography/geographic-information-science/overview) and take the first step toward a career in GIS.

--- SOURCE 5: How GIS is Revolutionizing Disaster Management and Response ---
URL: https://geographicbook.com/how-gis-is-revolutionizing-disaster-management-and-response/

SUMMARY:
GIS significantly enhances disaster management through real-time data, risk assessment, and resource allocation for effective responses.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- IMAGES ---

IMAGE 1:
URL: https://www.topbimcompany.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Disaster-management-cycle-with-GIS.png
DESCRIPTION: The graphic illustrates the disaster management cycle using GIS, featuring key elements such as predictive preparedness, smart mitigation, swift recovery, and rapid response, all interconnected in a dynamic flow around a colorful representation of geospatial data.

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://sp-uploads.s3.amazonaws.com/uploads/services/6562230/20230209182724_63e53b0c4d3fa_application_of_gis_in_disaster_managementpage0.jpg
DESCRIPTION: None

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://www.sgligis.com/wp-content/uploads/Featured-Image.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The collage highlights various aspects of disaster management and emergency response, showcasing flooding, damaged infrastructure, healthcare workers in protective gear, disease mapping, and the power of GIS technology in addressing natural disasters like hurricanes.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://i1.wp.com/www.igismap.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/03/Disaster-manage.jpg?fit=750,396&ssl=1
DESCRIPTION: The illustration features a stylized urban landscape on the left, accompanied by detailed maps on the right that analyze the impact of flooding and travel times in the Horn of Africa, highlighting the role of GIS in disaster management.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://mappitall.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/12/image1-1.png
DESCRIPTION: A digital map displays various disaster warnings, such as tsunami, tornado, and hurricane alerts, using color-coded overlays across North America, with GIS tools and layers for weather and hazard monitoring.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: GIS applications will broaden to include advanced predictive modeling, real-time data integration, and crowd-sourced information, enhancing disaster preparedness and response capabilities.
Future GIS systems will integrate AI, IoT, and blockchain to optimize data sharing, improve predictive accuracy, and facilitate timely decision-making during disasters.

Collaboration with local communities and organizations will enhance resource mobilization, improve situational awareness, and ensure tailored disaster management strategies that address specific local needs.
Training initiatives will focus on equipping emergency responders and community members with GIS skills, ensuring effective use of technology in disaster management and recovery efforts.
Public awareness campaigns will educate communities on GIS's role in disaster management, fostering resilience and encouraging participation in preparedness and response activities.
